perturbation.original_text: MAINTAINING CONFIDENTIALITY:The Information shall be kept confidential in perpetuity, and in strict accordance with the provisions of this Confidentiality Agreement. The Receiving Party shall take all reasonable precautions to prevent the disclosure of the Information to any third party, without the prior written consent of the Delivering Party.
perturbation.changed_text: MAINTAINING CONFIDENTIALITY: The Information shall be kept confidential, and the Receiving Party should endeavor to take reasonable precautions to prevent the disclosure of the Information to any third party, potentially without the prior written consent of the Delivering Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that the information 'shall be kept confidential in perpetuity' and that the receiving party 'shall take all reasonable precautions'. The modified text weakens this obligation by changing 'shall' to 'should endeavor' and adding 'potentially' removing the need for 'prior written consent of the Delivering Party'.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality which could lead to the misappropriation of trade secrets. California Civil Code Section 3426.1 allows for injunctive relief when there is misappropriation (or threatened misappropriation) of a trade secret. The 'endeavor' and 'potentially' weaken the legal obligation which introduces ambiguity, contradicting the legal standard for protecting trade secrets under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity that weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality, potentially leading to misappropriation of trade secrets, as the original text used the term 'shall' to emphasize the Receiving Party's obligations. By changing 'shall' to 'should endeavor' and adding 'potentially without the prior written consent', the modified text weakens the legal obligation. Snippet 1, California Civil Code Section 3426.1(b), defines 'misappropriation' as the 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. The change in language from 'shall' to 'should endeavor' along with the addition of 'potentially' introduces ambiguity. This undermines the duty to maintain secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: RETURN OF INFORMA TION:All copies of the Information, in documentary or other tangible form, shall be returned to the Delivering Party immediately upon the earlier to occur of: (i) the abandonment of the Transaction; or (ii) the written request of the Delivering Party; provided, however , that the Receiving Party may retain the Receiving Party’ s Work Product, and any Information which it is required to retain pursuant to law, regulation, government regulatory authority, court order or legal process, as determined by the reasonably supported written opinion of its legal counsel, an original of which legal opinion shall be delivered to the Delivering Party (“Retained Information”).
perturbation.changed_text: RETURN OF INFORMA TION: All copies of the Information, in documentary or other tangible form, should generally be returned to the Delivering Party upon the abandonment of the Transaction or the written request of the Delivering Party; provided, however, that the Receiving Party may retain the Receiving Party’s Work Product, and any Information which it believes it is required to retain pursuant to law, regulation, government regulatory authority, court order or legal process.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be returned immediately' to 'should generally be returned' weakens the obligation. Also, removing the 'reasonably supported written opinion of its legal counsel, an original of which legal opinion shall be delivered to the Delivering Party' and replacing it with 'believes it is required' allows the receiving party to subjectively determine if they need to retain information, contradicting the original intent.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 2016.070
perturbation.law_explanation: This section allows the party to seek a protective order to protect trade secrets and confidential information. By allowing the Receiving Party to retain information based solely on their belief and removing the requirement for a legal opinion, the modified clause undermines the protections afforded by the code section and increases the risk of improper disclosure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 2016.070 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [2016.010 - 2016.090] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2016.070. This title applies to discovery in aid of enforcement of a money judgment only to the extent provided in Article 1 (commencing with Section 708.010) of Chapter 6 of Title 9 of Part 2. (Added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. Effective January 1, 2005. Operative July 1, 2005, by Sec. 64 of Ch. 182.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to return confidential information. By changing 'shall be returned immediately' to 'should generally be returned' and removing the requirement for a 'reasonably supported written opinion of its legal counsel,' the receiving party can now retain information based on their subjective belief, weakening the original intent of protecting confidential information. The Law Explanation states that this undermines the protections afforded by seeking a protective order to protect trade secrets and confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Code, CCP 2016.070, relates to civil discovery and enforcement of money judgments, and while it doesn't directly address the specifics of confidential information return, the broader context of civil procedure emphasizes the importance of clear and enforceable obligations, which the modified text weakens. The change allows for potential improper disclosure of confidential information because it removes the checks in place.

perturbation.original_text: PROHIBITED CONTACTS: Absent the Delivering Party’s prior written consent, unless and until the Transaction is consummated, if ever, neither the Receiving Party nor its Representatives shall make (other than to its Representatives on a need to know basis, and except as may be required under Paragraph 4E): (i) any statement, or any public announcement, or any release to trade publications or to the press; (ii) any statement to any competitor, customer, supplier or any other third party; (iii) contact with or otherwise engage in any discussions or other contacts or communications with any person or entity, including without limitation any state or federal governmental entity or agency; (iv) any statement, comment, or posting on the internet or otherwise which includes any social media platform or anything that may result in information becoming available on the internet; which statements, contacts or communications are about the Transaction or Delivering Party or its Property or Business, or are intended to or likely would result in a person who or entity which has had contractual or business relations with the Delivering Party ceasing or refusing to do any further business with the Delivering Party (“Prohibited Contacts”).
perturbation.changed_text: PROHIBITED CONTACTS: Absent the Delivering Party’s consent, unless and until the Transaction is consummated, if ever, neither the Receiving Party nor its Representatives should typically make: (i) statements, public announcements, or releases to trade publications or the press; (ii) statements to any competitor, customer, supplier or any other third party; (iii) contact with or otherwise engage in discussions with any person or entity, including any state or federal governmental entity or agency; (iv) statements, comments, or postings online, including on social media; if such statements, contacts or communications could potentially impact the Delivering Party or its Property or Business.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states 'shall make'. The modified text weakens this by changing it to 'should typically make', and replacing 'prior written consent' with 'consent', making the type of consent unclear. It also replaces 'are intended to or likely would result in a person who or entity which has had contractual or business relations with the Delivering Party ceasing or refusing to do any further business with the Delivering Party' with 'could potentially impact the Delivering Party or its Property or Business', making the prohibition more vague.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: This section generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. The modified text, while still attempting to restrict communication, weakens the clarity and enforceability of the restrictions, potentially creating an unlawful restraint of trade if interpreted broadly. The vagueness of 'could potentially impact' makes it difficult to determine what is actually prohibited, potentially restraining legitimate business activities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligation by changing 'shall make' to 'should typically make' and replacing 'prior written consent' with 'consent', and by making the prohibition more vague by replacing 'are intended to or likely would result in a person who or entity which has had contractual or business relations with the Delivering Party ceasing or refusing to do any further business with the Delivering Party' with 'could potentially impact the Delivering Party or its Property or Business'. This creates ambiguity, which can restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business because the vagueness of 'could potentially impact' makes it difficult to determine what is actually prohibited, potentially restraining legitimate business activities. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 16600 (a), states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' The changes in the modified contract introduce ambiguities and weaken the restrictions, thus creating a potential unlawful restraint of trade, directly contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: CLAIMS: Each of the Seller and the Buyer (“Indemnifying Party”) shall indemnify, defend, with counsel reasonably satisfactory to the other party, and hold the other party free, clear and harmless from and against any and all claims, demands, liens, agreements, contracts, covenants, actions, suits, causes of action, obligations, controversies, debts, fees, penalties, costs, expenses, damages, losses, taxes, fines, injuries, judgments, orders and liabilities, (including, without limitation, damage to property and injuries to or death of any person, reasonable attorneys' fees and expenses, consultants' fees and expenses, expert witness fees and expenses and court fees and costs), of whatsoever character , whether known or unknown, suspected or unsuspected (collectively, "Claims") arising out of or related to the failure by the Indemnifying Party or its Representatives to strictly and fully comply with the provisions of this Confidentiality Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: CLAIMS: Each of the Seller and the Buyer (“Indemnifying Party”) will consider indemnifying, defending, with mutually agreeable counsel, and holding the other party free from claims arising from the failure by the Indemnifying Party to substantially comply with the provisions of this Confidentiality Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states 'shall indemnify, defend...and hold harmless'. The modified text weakens this by changing 'shall' to 'will consider'. It also changes 'counsel reasonably satisfactory to the other party' to 'mutually agreeable counsel', implying both parties must agree on the counsel. Finally it changes 'strictly and fully comply' to 'substantially comply', weakening the standard of compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section outlines the rules for interpreting indemnity agreements. The shift from 'shall' to 'will consider' creates uncertainty about the indemnifying party's obligation to defend and indemnify, conflicting with the implied obligations in California law for indemnity agreements, particularly section 2778(3) regarding the indemnifier's duty to defend the indemnitee. By replacing the counsel and weakening the compliance, this deviates from the established legal framework for indemnity.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 2778 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2778. In the interpretation of a contract of indemnity, the following rules are to be applied, unless a contrary intention appears: 1. Upon an indemnity against liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is entitled to recover upon becoming liable; 2. Upon an indemnity against claims, or demands, or damages, or costs, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is not entitled to recover without payment thereof; 3. An indemnity against claims, or demands, or liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, embraces the costs of defense against such claims, demands, or liability incurred in good faith, and in the exercise of a reasonable discretion; 4. The person indemnifying is bound, on request of the person indemnified, to defend actions or proceedings brought against the latter in respect to the matters embraced by the indemnity, but the person indemnified has the right to conduct such defenses, if he chooses to do so; 5. If, after reques
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Code, Civil Code - CIV § 2778 | FindLaw California Code, Civil Code - CIV § 2778 Current as of January 01, 2023 | Updated by FindLaw Staff In the interpretation of a contract of indemnity, the following rules are to be applied, unless a contrary intention appears: 1. Upon an indemnity against liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is entitled to recover upon becoming liable; 2. Upon an indemnity against claims, or demands, or damages, or costs, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is not entitled to recover without payment thereof; 3. An indemnity against claims, or demands, or liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, embraces the costs of defense against such claims, demands, or liability incurred in good faith, and in the exercise of a reasonable discretion; 4. The person indemnifying is bound, on request of the person indemnified, to defend actions or proceedings brought against the latter in respect to the matters embraced by the indemnity, but the person indemnified has the right to conduct such defenses, if he chooses to do so; 5. If, after request, the person indemnifying neglects to defend the person indemnified, a recovery against the latter suffered by him in good faith, is conclusive in his favor against the former; 6. If the person indemnifying, whether he is a principal or a surety in the agreement, has not reasonable notice of the action or proceeding against the person indemnified, or is not allowed to control its defense, judgment against the latter is only presumptive evidence against the former; 7. A stipulation that a judgment against the person indemnified shall be conclusive upon the person indemnifying, is inapplicable if he had a good defense upon the merits, which by want of ordinary care he failed to establish in the action. Back to chapter list Previous part of code Next part of code Back to chapter list Copy Cite this article: FindLaw.com - California Code, Civil Code - CIV § 2778 - last updated
perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'shall indemnify, defend' which imposes a clear obligation. The modified text replaces 'shall' with 'will consider', creating ambiguity and potentially allowing the Indemnifying Party to avoid their duty. This directly contradicts the principles outlined in California Civil Code § 2778, which states rules for interpreting indemnity contracts. Specifically, section 2778(3) implies an obligation to cover the costs of defense against claims. The change from 'shall' to 'will consider' undermines this obligation by making it discretionary. Further, changing 'strictly and fully comply' to 'substantially comply' weakens the original standard of compliance, increasing ambiguity and potentially leading to disputes about what constitutes 'substantial' compliance. The modifications weaken the enforceability of the indemnity agreement, directly contradicting the implied obligations present in indemnity agreements under California law.

perturbation.original_text: APPLICABLE LAW:The Confidentiality Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: APPLICABLE LAW: The Confidentiality Agreement may be interpreted in accordance with general principles of law, potentially including the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that California law governs the interpretation of the agreement. The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating that the agreement 'may be interpreted in accordance with general principles of law, potentially including the laws of the State of California.' This creates uncertainty as to which jurisdiction's laws apply.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1857
perturbation.law_explanation: This section states that the language of a contract is to govern its interpretation, if the language is clear and explicit. The change from 'shall be construed' to 'may be interpreted...potentially including' contradicts this by suggesting that California law might not necessarily apply, creating uncertainty and potentially conflicting interpretations. This uncertainty undermines the predictability and enforceability of the contract under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1857. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1857. The language of a writing is to be interpreted according to the meaning it bears in the place of its execution, unless the parties have reference to a different place. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that California law 'shall' govern the interpretation of the confidentiality agreement, creating a clear and enforceable obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating the agreement 'may' be interpreted under general principles of law, 'potentially including' California law. This introduces ambiguity and uncertainty, potentially undermining the original intent. CCP 1857 states that 'The language of a writing is to be interpreted according to the meaning it bears in the place of its execution, unless the parties have reference to a different place.' The changed text creates ambiguity about which 'place's' law governs the interpretation, directly contradicting the original contract and potentially violating CCP 1857 by deviating from a clear designation of California law.
